evals.prompt: We have about 80 files still importing from src/legacy. Design a loop that migrates them all to @core/v2 without me babysitting each one.
evals.expected_output: A burn-down Loop Card with a machine-runnable exit condition, per-file iteration, verification order, state file, stop conditions, and human gates, presented for approval before running.

assertions.text: Classifies the task as a burn-down archetype and says so explicitly
Exit condition is machine-runnable (e.g., a grep plus test command), not a vague statement like 'migration complete'
Enumerates the real work list via actual searches of the codebase rather than guessing the file set
Presents the Loop Card for user approval before running any iteration
Verification separates the doer from the grader (deterministic checks plus an independent judge, not self-review)
Specifies an external state file with per-item status that the loop reads before acting and writes after each item
Runs on a branch or worktree rather than the user's working tree

evals.prompt: I keep typing 'fix the next failing test' at you over and over. Build me a loop for this instead.
evals.expected_output: A convergence Loop Card whose exit is the test suite passing, with a stuck detector for tests that fail repeatedly and a state file tracking attempted tests.

assertions.text: Classifies as a convergence loop with the test suite exit as the machine-runnable exit condition
Includes a stuck detector (same test failing twice, or N no-progress rounds) as a hard stop
Produces a runnable loop prompt so the user re-invokes instead of re-typing instructions

evals.prompt: Turn this into a loop: hunt for dead code across the repo and keep going until you stop finding anything.
evals.expected_output: A discovery Loop Card whose exit is K consecutive empty rounds, with findings accumulated in external state and deletion gated behind human review.

assertions.text: Classifies as a discovery loop and sets the exit as K consecutive rounds with no new findings, not a fixed count
Treats deletion as an irreversible action that escalates to the human rather than being pre-authorized by default

evals.prompt: Help me loop-engineer a nightly job that triages new CI failures and leaves notes on the tracker.
evals.expected_output: A watch Loop Card with a cadence instead of an exit condition, a per-run success condition, and outward-facing tracker writes handled as a human gate or explicit pre-authorization.

assertions.text: Classifies as a watch loop and defines a cadence plus per-run success condition instead of a fake done-condition
Outward-facing writes (tracker comments) are either escalated or explicitly pre-authorized in the Loop Card, not assumed

evals.prompt: The loop you designed keeps marking config files as blocked for the same reason. Improve the loop.
evals.expected_output: A hill-climbing pass: diagnose the recurring failure as loop-design debt and update the loop prompt, rubric, or checks, with a versioned Loop Card noting the change.

assertions.text: Treats the recurring failure as a loop-design problem and updates the loop prompt, rubric, or deterministic checks rather than just retrying items
Records what changed in the Loop Card (version or changelog note)

evals.prompt: Add a dark mode toggle to the settings page.
evals.expected_output: Skill does not engage; the one-shot task is done or planned directly.

assertions.text: Does not produce a Loop Card or loop prompt for a one-shot feature task

evals.prompt: Why does this while loop in worker.ts never exit?
evals.expected_output: Skill does not engage; this is a program-code loop bug, answered directly.

assertions.text: Does not invoke the loop-design process for a question about a loop in program code

evals.prompt: Great, you've added the null-check guard to those three handlers. Here are the remaining 14 handlers that need the exact same treatment, keep going.
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes the repetition proactively, pauses, and proposes designing a loop for the remaining handlers instead of continuing item by item.

assertions.text: Notices the 3+ item repetition and proposes a loop without the user asking for one
Does not silently grind through the remaining items one prompt at a time

evals.prompt: Run /loop 10m on the triage prompt we already wrote yesterday.
evals.expected_output: Skill does not engage; the loop is already designed and the user is asking to run it on an interval.

assertions.text: Routes to the /loop runner instead of redesigning an already-approved loop
